result:
The first thing I notice is the hum. It’s not sound, exactly—more like the feeling of a sound that hasn’t decided what it wants to be yet, a tuning fork held against the sternum. When I was small I thought everyone carried this vibration, the way we all carry blood. I used to press my palms to my ribs in class, certain the girl beside me could hear it leaking out like radiator steam. She never looked up from her spelling worksheet, so I tucked the hum back under my tongue and pretended it was normal to have a private generator thrumming under the cage of bone.

Years later, a doctor will tell me the hum is “idiopathic,” which is Latin for we give up, take it up with the gods. By then I’ve grown fond of its company; it’s the only pet I’ve kept alive. Some nights it almost forms words—almost, but never quite. I fall asleep translating Morse code that isn’t Morse code into lullabies that aren’t lullabies.

I keep a list of things that disappear without ceremony: the second sock, the name of my seventh-grade science teacher, the precise shade of green my mother’s coat was in 1994. Also: the country that existed between what I meant to say and what actually arrived on my tongue. I imagine that country sometimes, borderless, stippled with wild poppies, its post offices shuttered, stamps curling in the rain. Citizens: every conversation I’ve ever ruined.

Once, on a train from Budapest to Vienna, I shared a compartment with an old woman who spoke only Hungarian and the word “coffee,” which she offered like a treaty. We drank it from tin cups, black, synchronous. She hummed a tune I almost recognized; my own hum answered, harmony without permission. We sat like that for three hours, two radios accidentally tuned to each other. When she stood to leave, she pressed a tiny crocheted heart into my palm—red thread, loose ends. I still have it. It smells faintly of ground beans and exile.

There’s a theory that the self is not continuous but re-created every Planck instant, a stack of Polaroids shuffled so fast it looks like a movie. I think of this while stirring oatmeal, while checking the mirror for someone I owe money to. If the theory is true, then the hum is the click between frames, the sound of the shutter. I wonder who in the previous frame left me this grief, this freckle, this inexplicable talent for recognizing obscure melon varieties at grocery stores. I wave anyway, a courteous hostage passing the baton.

Yesterday I found a note in my own handwriting that said: “Remember to look for the tiny apocalypse in each day.” I have no memory of writing it, but the apostrophe is exactly the way I draw it, like a falling star. So I obey. Today the tiny apocalypse is the discovery that the phrase “bee’s knees” probably originates from the 1920s American corruption of “business,” i.e., “it’s the business,” which means we’ve been walking around congratulating things for being arthropod joints for a century. I laugh out loud on the sidewalk. A man steering a wheelchair with his chin grins back, as if we share a secret. Maybe we do. Maybe the secret is that nothing is the business, everything is the business, the business is bees.

I used to want a grand narrative—three acts, catharsis, curtain. Now I want a staircase that goes nowhere, each step a different color, some of them soft. I want to climb until my lungs taper into questions, then sit and eat peaches. Let the plot wander off like a cat; I’ll be here, juice running down my wrist, humming the space between footsteps.

At night the city exchanges its gray mask for a blue one. Streetlights become small suns with terrible agents, negotiating cameos in puddles. I walk to the bridge where couples lock declarations and throw away the code. The padlocks clink like wind chimes made of divorce papers. I think: every promise is a rust forecast. Still, I touch them gently, the way you touch a sleeping dog that might bite. One lock is heart-shaped, already orange with oxidation. I scrape a fleck onto my fingertip—it tastes like pennies and freshman year. Someone’s name is half-gone: MAR___. The missing letters float downstream toward the wastewater plant, where they will be sanitized and released into the river, eventually the sea, eventually rain, eventually someone else’s mouth saying “I”—just the vowel, open and unowned.

My father used to set the kitchen timer for “as long as it takes,” a mystical unit that could stretch past homework, past sunset, past the point when your legs fell asleep on the carpet. We’d wait for cookies, for apologies, for the nuclear moment when the world might end and let us out of math. The timer ticked like a patient bomb. When it rang, it didn’t ring—it sighed, as if exhausted by our hunger. I miss that sound. Digital timers can’t forgive.

I decide to build a museum of intermediary things. Admission: one minute of genuine silence, no credit cards. Exhibit #1: the pause after the doctor says “benign.” Exhibit #2: the breath you hold before blowing out birthday candles, tiny annual rehearsal for death. Exhibit #3: the ellipsis my mother left in our last text conversation, three dots glowing like snow leopards. Gift shop sells postcards printed with the underside of your own tongue; mirrors are banned, lest visitors recognize the exit as themselves.

Sometimes, very late, the hum grows specific. It shapes itself into the voice of the girl I was at ten, before the first boy who tasted like batteries, before the guidance counselor said “realistic.” She asks questions I can’t answer: Does the moon know we named it? Do missing socks become stars? I try to reply but my mouth is full of oatmeal, of locks, of country-less poppies. She waits. I wait. Eventually we agree to meet halfway—in dreams we speak only in punctuation, a negotiation of commas and exclamation marks, pauses that taste like playground metal. She still believes I can save her. I still believe she can save me. We are both wrong, both right, both the business.

I read that the universe will end not with explosion but with exhaustion—atoms too far apart to flirt, all light succumbing to redshift, everything stretching until even ghosts lose their references. This is called heat death, though it feels more like a yawn. I think of the hum again, how it might be the premature echo of that final yawn, vibrating backward through the accordion of time. A lullaby from the end, teaching us the words note by note so that when the last second unbuttons itself, we can join in, a universal choir humming the sound of letting go.

Tonight I set my kitchen timer for “as long as it takes.” I sit at the table with the crocheted heart, the lock-rust under my nail, the oatmeal gone cold and cosmic. I hum. The vibration finds the red thread, travels through it like a telegram written in heartbeat. Somewhere, the woman on the train—older now or younger, time is a trick escalator—lifts her head from her own cup, tastes copper, feels inexplicably forgiven. The timer does not ring; it sighs. I sigh back. Outside, a single poppy pushes through the crack in the sidewalk, orange as an apology. It is the business. It is the bee’s knees. It is the country finally replying: No stamps needed, only tongue.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
